name: Kumarila Bhatta
dates_notes: Mīmāṃsaka; dates fixed by Kataoka (2011, Kumārila on Truth, Omniscience, and Killing) and Bronkhorst (Karma 2011) by Kumārila's references to Dharmakīrti and being himself referenced by Śāntarakṣita; the late-seventh-century placement is now standard. The traditional identification with Bhaṭṭācārya, Maṇḍana's teacher, is doubted by modern scholarship; what is secure is that Maṇḍana writes from within Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā.
core_thesis: Kumārila Bhaṭṭa is the Mīmāṃsā realist who fixes the school's mature epistemological position and whose categories Vedānta both inherits and contests (Clooney 2022, "Mīmāṃsā as Introspective Literature and as Philosophy," engages the *Ślokavārttika* specifically on the introspective register, including the *Codanā-Sūtra* and *ātmavāda* sections). Three theses define the position. First, *svataḥ-prāmāṇya*: the validity of cognition is intrinsic and is not produced by an external warranting cognition; falsity, when it occurs, is extrinsic and requires a separate defeating cognition (*Śloka-Vārttika*, *Codanā-Sūtra* section, vv. 33–61, on Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.2). The position is the negative image of the Naiyāyika *parataḥ-prāmāṇya* and is the locus of one of the most consequential debates in classical Indian epistemology. Second, *abhāva* as an autonomous *pramāṇa*: the cognition of an absence (the absent jar in this place) is irreducible to perception or inference and requires a sixth *pramāṇa*, *anupalabdhi* (non-apprehension) (*Śloka-Vārttika*, *Abhāva-Pariccheda*, vv. 1–53). The full Bhāṭṭa list — *pratyakṣa*, *anumāna*, *upamāna*, *śabda*, *arthāpatti*, *anupalabdhi* — is given here in the form Advaita inherits. Third, *apauruṣeyatva*: the *Veda* is unauthored, eternal, and self-validating; the Naiyāyika *īśvara*-as-*āpta*-speaker doctrine is rejected as both unnecessary and incompatible with Vedic authority (*Śloka-Vārttika*, *Sambandha-Akṣepa-Parihāra* and *Vākya-Adhikaraṇa*, on MS 1.1.5). The Bhāṭṭa analysis of *śabda-pramāṇa* — sentence-meaning constructed via *abhihitānvaya* (the words first denote their meanings and then those meanings combine into a sentence-meaning) — is the standard Mīmāṃsā position against the Prābhākara *anvitābhidhāna* (*Tantra-Vārttika* on MS 1.2 and 2.1, *vākyādhikaraṇa*). The doctrinal architecture is the Mīmāṃsā framework Maṇḍana inherits and modifies in service of Advaita; the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* debate that *Brahma-Siddhi* prosecutes hinges on the Bhāṭṭa analysis of *karma* and *jñāna* (cf. *Tantra-Vārttika* on MS 1.2.1 ff.).

engaged_works.title: Shloka-Varttika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored verse-commentary on the first pāda (Tarka-Pāda) of Śabara's Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra-Bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary: Verse-commentary (*vārttika*) on Śabara's *Bhāṣya* on the first *pāda* of the *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* (Clooney 2022 reads the *Codanā-Sūtra* and *ātmavāda* sections as constitutive of Mīmāṃsā's introspective register) — the *Tarka-Pāda*, MS 1.1.1–32. The *Tarka-Pāda* is the philosophical foundation of Mīmāṃsā: it treats the definition of *dharma*, the authority of *Veda*, the analysis of *codanā* (injunctive force), the structure of *śabda-pramāṇa*, and the response to opponents. Kumārila uses this exegetical occasion to construct a complete Mīmāṃsā epistemology. The *Codanā-Sūtra* section (vv. 33–185, on MS 1.1.2) gives the *svataḥ-prāmāṇya* doctrine; the *Pratyakṣa-Sūtra* section (on MS 1.1.4) gives the Bhāṭṭa theory of perception; the *Sambandha-Akṣepa-Parihāra* (on MS 1.1.5) gives the doctrine of *śabda-artha-sambandha* as eternal; the *Vana-Vāda* (in the *Ākṛti-Vāda*-cluster, on MS 1.1.5) gives the analysis of forest-perception that grounds the Mīmāṃsā ontology of perceived universals; the *Ātmā-Vāda* (within the *Śūnya-Vāda* and *Nirālambana-Vāda* refutations, on MS 1.1.5) gives the long argument for an enduring *ātman* against Buddhist *kṣaṇika-vāda* and *vijñāna-vāda*. The text is the Bhāṭṭa school's central philosophical document and the most exhaustively studied Mīmāṃsā work in late-medieval Indian philosophy. Pārthasārathi Miśra's *Nyāya-Ratnākara* and Sucarita Miśra's *Kāśikā* are the two principal sub-commentaries.

engaged_works.title: Tantra-Varttika
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Kumārila's mature period; treats MS 1.2 through 3.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored prose-and-verse commentary on Śabara's Bhāṣya on MS 1.2 through 3.
engaged_works.summary: Mixed prose-and-verse commentary on Śabara's *Bhāṣya* on *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* 1.2 through 3. Where the *Śloka-Vārttika* establishes Bhāṭṭa epistemology, the *Tantra-Vārttika* prosecutes the school's hermeneutic doctrine: the analysis of *vidhi* (injunction) (on MS 1.2.1–18, *vidhy-adhikaraṇa*), *arthavāda* (subordinate explanatory passages) (on MS 1.2.7), *mantra* (on MS 1.2.31–53), and *nāmadheya* (technical names) (on MS 1.4); the principles of *vinīyoga* (on MS 3.1–3.6); the analysis of conflict-resolution principles among injunctions (on MS 3.3, *bādha-adhikaraṇa*). The *Tantra-Vārttika* is the more textually-dense of the two *vārttikas* and is where the Bhāṭṭa school's theory of *śabda-bodha* — sentence-cognition via *abhihitānvaya* — is most fully developed (on MS 1.2.31 ff. and 2.1.1–4). The work also contains the Mīmāṃsā polemic against Buddhist and Cārvāka denial of Vedic authority (on MS 1.3, *smṛti-adhikaraṇa*), and the realist defence of *jāti* and *sambandha* that the Naiyāyika tradition partly shares (on MS 1.3.30–35).
